title: Triiodothyroacetic acid exerts tissue-specific thyromimetic effects without tachycardia and hyperthermia in male mice
author: Beate Herrmann
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Thyroid hormones regulate metabolism, development, and physiological homeostasis across virtually all organ systems through binding to nuclear thyroid hormone receptors. The thyroid hormone analog 3,3',5-triiodothyroacetic acid (TRIAC) has emerged as a promising therapeutic candidate for thyroid hormone transporter deficiencies (Allan-Herndon-Dudley Syndrome) or thyroid hormone resistance. Although TRIAC shows therapeutic promise for these conditions, comprehensive characterization of its effects across different organ systems has not been systematically performed in mice. As this knowledge is critical for determining TRIAC's safety profile and therapeutic advantages over conventional thyroid hormone therapy, we administered TRIAC to wild-type mice for 14 days and assessed molecular, and physiological responses across multiple organ systems. TRIAC treatment reduced circulating thyroxine levels and induced robust upregulation of hepatic thyroid hormone-responsive genes including Dio1 and Me1, demonstrating classical thyromimetic activity. Most interestingly, cardiovascular analysis revealed normal blood pressure and heart rate, and no signs of cardiac hypertrophy despite specific molecular changes in cardiac gene expression. On the metabolic level, we surprisingly did not observe any effect on brown fat thermogenesis or body temperature, while in muscle Ucp3 and Gpd2 were reduced, suggesting possible issues with mitochondrial functions.  Taken together, our findings demonstrate that TRIAC exhibits tissue-selective thyromimetic activity, producing some metabolic responses while avoiding classical hyperthyroid manifestations such as tachycardia or hyperthermia. The tissue-selective nature of TRIAC action supports its potential as a safer therapeutic alternative in thyroid hormone resistance conditions as compared to thyroxine.
